narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school only to have your application turned away feels like a door slamming shut just as you're reaching for the handle. Our prediction model looked at your profile and estimated a 69.9% chance you'd struggle to pass the bar exam down the line, painting a picture of a path with more hurdles than smooth sailing. What stood out most was your Year 3 decile of 1, the lowest rung in your school, which screamed the loudest signal of potential trouble ahead, even more than your Year 1 decile of 2. Your LSAT score of 35 didn't help either—it was another strong pull toward the tougher side of things, suggesting the analytical demands of law might not play to your strengths as we hoped. Layered on top was your middle family income bracket, which the model saw as limiting access to the extra support that often makes the difference for bar prep. Your white background and male gender factored in too, aligning with patterns we've seen tip toward higher failure risks in similar profiles. Even your solid undergraduate GPA of 2.9 and full-time student status couldn't outweigh these, as they nudged the odds further against passing. It's like your story came together as one where early academic stumbles snowballed, and without standout test scores or other buffers, the model worried you'd hit a wall at the bar. This isn't about your worth—it's a data-driven snapshot urging you to build those weaker spots first. Imagine retaking the LSAT or bolstering your upper-year grades; those could rewrite this story entirely. You've got the drive to aim for law—use this as your plot twist to come back stronger. We're rooting for your next chapter.
